::: {.callout-note}
**Inference labs** use the five-step template:
Hypothesis → Visualise → Assumptions → Conduct → Conclude.
:::

## Learning objectives

- Choose between Student's and Welch's *t*-test, and between paired
  and independent-samples tests.
- Compute Cohen's *d* and Hedges' *g* as effect sizes.
- Report a two-sample comparison with point estimate, CI, and effect
  size.

## Prerequisites

Lab 3.4.

## Background

The two-sample *t*-test compares the means of two independent groups.
Student's version assumes equal variances across the groups; Welch's
does not. In most applied settings Welch's is the safer default — it
behaves nearly as well as Student's when variances are actually equal
and much better when they are not. The `t.test()` default in R is
Welch.

When the same subjects are measured twice, the samples are paired and
the two measurements are correlated. Ignoring the pairing inflates the
standard error and lowers power. Pairing is handled by computing the
within-subject difference and running a one-sample *t*-test on it.

Effect sizes remove units from the comparison. Cohen's *d* is the
standardised mean difference: the mean difference divided by the
pooled standard deviation. Hedges' *g* applies a small-sample
correction. Both are essential alongside a *p*-value — a large *d* at
a small *n* will often produce a non-significant *p*, but the *d* is
still a warning that a larger study might find a real effect.

## Setup


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(tidyverse)
library(effectsize)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))
```
:::


## 1. Hypothesis

Scenario A (independent): mean systolic blood pressure in treatment
vs placebo.

- H0: μ_trt = μ_pla, H1: μ_trt ≠ μ_pla, α = 0.05.

Scenario B (paired): SBP before and after a four-week intervention in
the same 30 patients.

- H0: mean within-subject change = 0, H1: ≠ 0.

## 2. Visualise


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
n <- 40
trt <- rnorm(n, mean = 135, sd = 12)
pla <- rnorm(n, mean = 142, sd = 12)
df_ind <- tibble(
  arm = rep(c("placebo", "treatment"), each = n),
  sbp = c(pla, trt)
)
```
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
df_ind |>
  ggplot(aes(arm, sbp, fill = arm)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, colour = "grey30") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "SBP (mmHg)") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](lab_week4_session1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){width=576}
:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
n2 <- 30
pre  <- rnorm(n2, 148, 10)
post <- pre - rnorm(n2, 5, 7)  # true mean drop of 5
df_pair <- tibble(id = seq_len(n2), pre = pre, post = post) |>
  mutate(delta = post - pre)
```
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
df_pair |>
  pivot_longer(c(pre, post), names_to = "time", values_to = "sbp") |>
  mutate(time = factor(time, levels = c("pre", "post"))) |>
  ggplot(aes(time, sbp, group = id)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "SBP (mmHg)")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](lab_week4_session1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){width=576}
:::
:::


## 3. Assumptions

Independent *t*: approximate normality of the sample means; Welch's
does not require equal variances. Paired *t*: approximate normality
of the within-subject differences.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
df_pair |>
  ggplot(aes(sample = delta)) +
  stat_qq() + stat_qq_line(colour = "firebrick") +
  labs(x = "Theoretical", y = "Sample (pair differences)")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](lab_week4_session1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=480}
:::
:::


## 4. Conduct

### Two-sample (Welch)


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
tt_welch <- t.test(sbp ~ arm, data = df_ind, var.equal = FALSE)
tt_welch
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

	Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  sbp by arm
t = 2.9095, df = 72.3, p-value = 0.004807
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group placebo and group treatment is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
  2.655371 14.209770
sample estimates:
  mean in group placebo mean in group treatment 
               142.9581                134.5256 
```


:::
:::


### Two-sample Student (for comparison)


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
tt_student <- t.test(sbp ~ arm, data = df_ind, var.equal = TRUE)
tt_student
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

	Two Sample t-test

data:  sbp by arm
t = 2.9095, df = 78, p-value = 0.004715
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group placebo and group treatment is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
  2.662545 14.202597
sample estimates:
  mean in group placebo mean in group treatment 
               142.9581                134.5256 
```


:::
:::


### Paired


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
tt_paired <- t.test(df_pair$post, df_pair$pre, paired = TRUE)
tt_paired
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

	Paired t-test

data:  df_pair$post and df_pair$pre
t = -2.4747, df = 29, p-value = 0.01943
alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -5.7941048 -0.5505107
sample estimates:
mean difference 
      -3.172308 
```


:::
:::


### Effect size


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
d_ind    <- cohens_d(sbp ~ arm, data = df_ind)
g_ind    <- hedges_g(sbp ~ arm, data = df_ind)
d_paired <- cohens_d(df_pair$post, df_pair$pre, paired = TRUE)
list(d_ind = d_ind, g_ind = g_ind, d_paired = d_paired)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
$d_ind
Cohen's d |       95% CI
------------------------
0.65      | [0.20, 1.10]

- Estimated using pooled SD.
$g_ind
Hedges' g |       95% CI
------------------------
0.64      | [0.20, 1.09]

- Estimated using pooled SD.
$d_paired
Cohen's d |         95% CI
--------------------------
-0.45     | [-0.82, -0.07]
```


:::
:::


## 5. Concluding statement

> **Independent comparison.** Mean SBP was
> 134.5 mmHg (SD 14.7) in the
> treatment arm and 143 (SD
> 11) in placebo. A Welch's *t*-test gave
> *t*(72.3) =
> 2.91, *p* =
> 0.0048, mean difference
> 8.4 mmHg (95% CI
> -14.2 to
> -2.7). Cohen's *d* =
> 0.65 (Hedges' *g* =
> 0.64), a small-to-medium effect.

> **Paired comparison.** The within-subject change
> (post − pre) had mean -3.2 mmHg (SD
> 7); paired *t*-test *t*(29)
> = -2.47, *p* =
> 0.019, 95% CI
> -5.8 to
> -0.6.

The effect size is the number that generalises. A *p*-value tells
you whether to reject; *d* and its CI tell you how much.

::: {.notes}
Insist that students always look at the paired-difference Q-Q plot.
The before/after line plot is pretty; the Q-Q plot is diagnostic.
:::

## Common pitfalls

- Running an independent-samples *t* on paired data and losing power.
- Reporting Student's *t* in the era of Welch; there is rarely a good
  reason.
- Reporting *p* without *d* (or an equivalent effect size).
- Confusing effect size with statistical significance.

## Further reading

- Delacre M et al. (2017). *Why psychologists should by default use
  Welch's t-test*.
- Cohen J. *Statistical Power Analysis for the Behavioral Sciences*.
